message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 25
Recent changes: Green phase added NO production code — "steel sword ench 9 → 400" confirmed high-enchantment 50% clause works for non-dragon material; already implemented.

Refactor while keeping all tests green. Evaluate naming first, then Four Rules, APP mass before/after. No code changed; confirm cleanliness or make a small justified improvement only. Do not implement ahead (deductible-per-damage-event multi-item, cap exhaustion across claims, multiple-same-type items, error cases not yet tested).

Run autonomously, return after completion.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by reading the implementation and test files to evaluate the current state.
Let me run the tests to confirm the current state is green.

content.text:
All 25 tests pass. Now let me evaluate the code.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Walking the public and internal names:

- `runScenario` — accurately describes processing a scenario of steps. Good.
- `quotePremium`, `claimResult` — clear intent.
- `policyBasePremium`, `typeBasePremium`, `surchargeForItem`, `itemSurcharges` — all reveal intent precisely.
- `policyModifierRate` / `policyModifierAmount` — clear.
- `qualifiesForHalfReimbursement`, `reimbursableAmount`, `damagePayout` — clear.
- `insuranceSum`, `countByType`, `isComponent`, `isLoyal` — clear.
- Constants (`BASE_PREMIUM`, `INSURANCE_VALUE`, `DEDUCTIBLE`, `HIGH_DAMAGE_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD`, etc.) — descriptive and accurate.

One observation: `qualifiesForHalfReimbursement` is the high-enchantment claim clause. The recent green confirmed it applies to ANY high-enchantment item (steel sword ench 9), not just dragon material — and the name does NOT imply dragon-specificity, so it correctly reveals the now-confirmed broader intent. No rename needed. **Naming is clean.**

## Step 2: APP Mass — focus area

Let me look for a genuine, small improvement. Examining `runScenario` (the only place with mutation + branching) and `damagePayout`.

In `damagePayout`, `policyItems.find(...)` returns `Item | undefined`, which flows into `qualifiesForHalfReimbursement(item: Item | undefined)`. This is fine. The current code is well-decomposed.

Let me check the one spot of duplication/clarity: the `else` branch in `runScenario` reads a fallback `?? []` for an unknown policy index. That is defensive for an untested error case (claim references item not in policy) — but since it is already present and tested paths don't exercise it, I'll leave it (removing it would risk the untested path and isn't an improvement to tested behavior).

Let me look closely for a legitimate small win. Consider `claimResult`:

```
const cap = 2 * insuranceSum(policyItems);
const payout = step.incident.damages.reduce(...);
return { payout: Math.floor(payout), remainingCap: cap - payout };
```

This is clean. `2` is a magic number (cap is 2× insurance sum). There's a named threshold pattern everywhere else in the file; `2` here is an unnamed business rule (the cap multiplier). Extracting it as a named constant improves **Rule 2 (reveals intent)** and matches the file's established convention (`DEDUCTIBLE`, `HALF_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE`, etc.). This is a justified, small, intent-revealing change.

## APP Mass before (the `claimResult` line touched)

`const cap = 2 * insuranceSum(policyItems);`
- assignment (binding `cap`): the literal `2` (constant: 1), `insuranceSum` invocation (2), `*` invocation (2), `policyItems` binding (1) → constants 1 + invocations 4 + binding 1 = 6 for the expression.

Introducing `CAP_MULTIPLIER = 2` constant: declaration adds 1 constant + 1 binding = 2; usage replaces literal `2` (constant 1) with binding `CAP_MULTIPLIER` (binding 1) — net mass at the call site unchanged. Total mass change: +2 (the new declaration). This is the documented trade-off where **Rule 2 (reveals intent) trumps APP**: a +2 mass delta buys a named business rule consistent with the rest of the file.

Let me apply it.

content.text:
All 25 tests still pass.

## Refactoring Complete

**Naming Evaluation (first priority):** All names reviewed and confirmed clean. Notably, `qualifiesForHalfReimbursement` correctly reveals the now-confirmed broader intent — the recent Green phase verified the high-enchantment 50% clause applies to any material (steel sword ench 9), and the name carries no dragon-specificity, so no rename was warranted.

**Four Rules of Simple Design:**
- Rule 1 (Tests Pass): 25/25 green before and after.
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): One improvement applied — extracted the unnamed cap multiplier literal `2` into a named constant `CAP_MULTIPLIER`, matching the file's established convention of named business-rule constants (`DEDUCTIBLE`, `HALF_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE`, etc.). The "cap is twice the insurance sum" rule is now self-documenting.
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): No duplication found; the type/value lookup tables and reduce-based aggregations are each single-sourced.
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): No unnecessary abstractions. Each helper maps to one spec concept.

**APP Mass change:** Net +2 (one constant declaration: 1 constant + 1 binding). The call site is unchanged (literal `2` → binding `CAP_MULTIPLIER`, both mass 1). This is the documented case where **Rule 2 trumps APP** — a +2 mass cost buys a named business rule consistent with the rest of the file.

**Did not implement ahead:** Left the 11 `it.todo` cases untouched (deductible-per-damage-event multi-item, cap exhaustion across claims, multiple-same-type items, error cases). The defensive `?? []` fallback in `runScenario` was left in place rather than removed, since the unknown-policy error path is not yet tested.

**Tests:** All passing.

File: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_06-28-45_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
